title: Arrays
content:
Definition: An array is a linear data structure that stores elements of the same type in contiguous memory locations, accessed via zero-based indices.

Key Points:
- O(1) access time using indices.
- Fixed-size (static) or resizable (dynamic).
- Pros: Fast access, cache-friendly.
- Cons: Costly middle insertions/deletions.

Use Cases:
- Storing lists (e.g., scores, names).
- Matrix operations.
- Hash table foundation.

Complexity:
- Access: O(1)
- Insert/Delete (end): O(1)
- Insert/Delete (middle): O(n)
- Search: O(n) unsorted, O(log n) sorted

Subtypes:
1. Static Array:
   Definition: A fixed-size array allocated at compile-time with a set capacity.
   Diagram:
   ```
   Index:  0    1    2
          +----+----+----+
   Value: | 4  | 2  | 7  |
          +----+----+----+
   ```
2. Dynamic Array:
   Definition: A resizable array (e.g., C++ vector) that adjusts capacity dynamically.
   Diagram:
   ```
   Index:  0    1    2    3
          +----+----+----+----+
   Value: | 4  | 2  | 7  | 1  |
          +----+----+----+----+
   ```
3. Multidimensional Array:
   Definition: An array of arrays, typically used for matrices (e.g., 2D grids).
   Diagram:
   ```
          +----+----+----+
   Row0: | 1  | 2  | 3  |
          +----+----+----+
   Row1: | 4  | 5  | 6  |
          +----+----+----+
   ```
4. Sparse Array:
   Definition: An array with mostly zero/empty elements, storing only non-zero values.
   Diagram:
   ```
   Index:  0    1    2    3    4    5
          +----+----+----+----+----+----+
   Value: | 0  | 0  | 7  | 0  | 0  | 9  |
          +----+----+----+----+----+----+
   ```

title: Strings
content:
Definition: A string is a sequence of characters, typically stored as an array or specialized object, used for text representation.

Key Points:
- Supports concatenation, substring extraction, pattern matching.
- Pros: Essential for text processing.
- Cons: Immutable strings costly to modify.

Use Cases:
- Text parsing (e.g., JSON).
- Data validation (e.g., emails).
- String algorithms.

Complexity:
- Access: O(1)
- Concatenation: O(n)
- Search (substring): O(n*m) naive, O(n+m) KMP

Subtypes:
1. Immutable String:
   Definition: A string that cannot be modified after creation, ensuring thread safety.
   Diagram:
   ```
   Index:  0    1    2
          +----+----+----+
   Value: | a  | b  | c  |
          +----+----+----+
   ```
2. Mutable String:
   Definition: A string that can be modified in-place, often using a buffer.
   Diagram:
   ```
   Index:  0    1    2    3
          +----+----+----+----+
   Value: | a  | b  | c  | d  |
          +----+----+----+----+
   ```
3. Substring:
   Definition: A contiguous portion of a string extracted from specific indices.
   Diagram:
   ```
   Index:  1    2
          +----+----+
   Value: | b  | c  |
          +----+----+
   ```
4. Palindrome:
   Definition: A string that reads the same forward and backward (e.g., 'radar').
   Diagram:
   ```
   Index:  0    1    2    3    4
          +----+----+----+----+----+
   Value: | r  | a  | d  | a  | r  |
          +----+----+----+----+----+
   ```

title: Linked Lists
content:
Definition: A linked list is a linear data structure where elements are stored in nodes, each containing data and a pointer to the next node.

Key Points:
- Dynamic size, no pre-allocation.
- O(1) insertion/deletion at known positions.
- Pros: Flexible size.
- Cons: O(n) access, extra pointer memory.

Use Cases:
- Stacks, queues, graphs.
- Dynamic memory management.
- Undo functionality.

Complexity:
- Access: O(n)
- Insert/Delete (head): O(1)
- Insert/Delete (tail/middle): O(n)
- Search: O(n)

Subtypes:
1. Singly Linked List:
   Definition: Each node points to the next, forming a unidirectional chain.
   Diagram:
   ```
   [ 1 | * ]→[ 2 | * ]→[ 3 | * ]→null
   ```
2. Doubly Linked List:
   Definition: Each node has pointers to next and previous nodes for bidirectional traversal.
   Diagram:
   ```
   null←[ * | 1 | * ]↔[ * | 2 | * ]↔[ * | 3 | * ]→null
   ```
3. Circular Linked List:
   Definition: Last node points to first, forming a loop.
   Diagram:
   ```
   [ 1 | * ]→[ 2 | * ]→[ 3 | * ]
    ^                       |
    +-----------------------+
   ```
4. Circular Doubly Linked List:
   Definition: Doubly linked list where last node links to first bidirectionally.
   Diagram:
   ```
   [ * | 1 | * ]↔[ * | 2 | * ]↔[ * | 3 | * ]
    ^                                 |
    +---------------------------------+
   ```

title: Stacks
content:
Definition: A stack is a linear data structure following the Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) principle, where elements are added/removed from the top.

Key Points:
- Operations: push, pop, peek.
- Pros: O(1) push/pop.
- Cons: Top-only access.

Use Cases:
- Call stack management.
- Expression evaluation.
- Backtracking.

Complexity:
- Push/Pop/Peek: O(1)
- Search: O(n)

Subtypes:
1. Array Stack:
   Definition: Stack using a fixed or dynamic array.
   Diagram:
   ```
          +----+
   Top→   | 3  |
          +----+
          | 2  |
          +----+
          | 1  |
          +----+
   ```
2. Linked Stack:
   Definition: Stack using a linked list with top as head.
   Diagram:
   ```
   Top→[ 3 | * ]→[ 2 | * ]→[ 1 | * ]→null
   ```
3. Min Stack:
   Definition: Stack tracking minimum element at any time.
   Diagram:
   ```
          +----+ Min: 1
   Top→   | 3  |
          +----+
          | 2  |
          +----+
          | 1  |
          +----+
   ```

title: Queues
content:
Definition: A queue is a linear data structure following the First-In-First-Out (FIFO) principle, with additions at rear and removals at front.

Key Points:
- Operations: enqueue, dequeue, front.
- Pros: O(1) enqueue/dequeue.
- Cons: Limited access.

Use Cases:
- Task scheduling.
- BFS.
- Buffering.

Complexity:
- Enqueue/Dequeue: O(1)
- Front: O(1)
- Search: O(n)

Subtypes:
1. Simple Queue:
   Definition: Basic linear queue with straightforward enqueue/dequeue.
   Diagram:
   ```
   Front→[ 1 ] [ 2 ] [ 3 ]←Rear
   ```
2. Circular Queue:
   Definition: Queue reusing space by connecting rear to front.
   Diagram:
   ```
   [ 3 ] [   ] [   ]
    ^               |
    +---------------+ [ 1 ] [ 2 ]
   ```
3. Priority Queue:
   Definition: Queue dequeuing elements by priority.
   Diagram:
   ```
   Front→[ 1 ] [ 2 ] [ 3 ]←Rear (priority)
   ```
4. Double Ended Queue (Deque):
   Definition: Queue allowing addition/removal at both ends.
   Diagram:
   ```
   ←[ 1 ] [ 2 ] [ 3 ]→
   ```

title: Deques
content:
Definition: A deque (double-ended queue) is a linear data structure allowing insertion/deletion at both front and rear ends.

Key Points:
- Operations: pushFront, pushBack, popFront, popBack.
- Pros: Flexible access.
- Cons: Complex implementation.

Use Cases:
- Sliding window algorithms.
- Undo/redo.
- Scheduling.

Complexity:
- Push/Pop: O(1)
- Access: O(n)

Subtypes:
1. Input-Restricted Deque:
   Definition: Deque restricted to insertions at one end.
   Diagram:
   ```
   ←[ 1 ] [ 2 ] [ 3 ]
   ```
2. Output-Restricted Deque:
   Definition: Deque restricted to removals at one end.
   Diagram:
   ```
   [ 1 ] [ 2 ] [ 3 ]→
   ```
3. Array Deque:
   Definition: Deque using a circular array.
   Diagram:
   ```
   [ 3 ] [   ] [   ]
    ^               |
    +---------------+ [ 1 ] [ 2 ]
   ```
4. Linked List Deque:
   Definition: Deque using a doubly linked list.
   Diagram:
   ```
   null←[ * | 1 | * ]↔[ * | 2 | * ]↔[ * | 3 | * ]→null
   ```

title: Trees
content:
Definition: A tree is a hierarchical data structure with nodes, each having a value and references to child nodes, starting from a single root.

Key Points:
- Operations: insertion, deletion, traversal (inorder, preorder, postorder).
- Pros: Hierarchical organization.
- Cons: Unbalanced trees degrade performance.

Use Cases:
- File systems.
- Database indexing.
- Expression trees.

Complexity:
- Search/Insert/Delete: O(h) (h = height, O(log n) balanced, O(n) unbalanced)
- Traversal: O(n)

Subtypes:
1. Binary Tree:
   Definition: A tree where each node has at most two children (left, right).
   Diagram:
   ```
           [ 1 ]
          /     \
         /       \
      [ 2 ]     [ 3 ]
     /     \
   [ 4 ] [ 5 ]
   ```
2. Binary Search Tree (BST):
   Definition: A binary tree where left subtree < node < right subtree.
   Diagram:
   ```
           [ 5 ]
          /     \
         /       \
      [ 3 ]     [ 7 ]
     /     \
   [ 2 ] [ 4 ]
   ```
3. AVL Tree:
   Definition: A self-balancing BST with subtree height difference at most 1.
   Diagram:
   ```
           [ 5 ]
          /     \
         /       \
      [ 3 ]     [ 7 ]
     /
   [ 2 ]
   ```
4. Red-Black Tree:
   Definition: A self-balancing BST with color properties for balance.
   Diagram:
   ```
           [ 5(B) ]
          /        \
         /          \
      [ 3(R) ]    [ 7(B) ]
   ```
5. B-Tree:
   Definition: A self-balancing tree with multiple keys per node, used in databases.
   Diagram:
   ```
         [ 2 , 4 ]
        /   |      \
      [ 1 ] [ 3 ] [ 5 , 6 ]
   ```
6. B+ Tree:
   Definition: A B-tree variant where leaf nodes store data, used in file systems.
   Diagram:
   ```
         [ 4 ]
        /     \
   [1,2,3] [5,6,7]
   ```
7. N-ary Tree:
   Definition: A tree where nodes can have multiple children.
   Diagram:
   ```
           [ 1 ]
         /  |  \
        /   |   \
      [ 2 ] [ 3 ] [ 4 ]
   ```
8. Trie:
   Definition: A tree for storing strings, each node representing a character.
   Diagram:
   ```
           [ * ]
          /     \
         /       \
       [ t ]     [ * ]
      /           |
    [ o ]        [ e ]
                  |
                 [ a ]
   ```
9. Segment Tree:
   Definition: A binary tree for range queries (e.g., sum) over an array.
   Diagram:
   ```
           [ 16 ]
          /      \
         /        \
      [ 4 ]      [ 12 ]
     /     \     /      \
   [ 1 ] [ 3 ] [ 5 ]  [ 7 ]
   ```
10. Fenwick Tree:
    Definition: A tree-like structure for cumulative queries using bit operations.
    Diagram:
    ```
   [ 1 , 2 , 3 , 4 ]
   ```

title: Heaps
content:
Definition: A heap is a complete binary tree where each node satisfies the heap property (e.g., parent <= children for min-heap).

Key Points:
- Operations: insert, extract-min/max, peek.
- Pros: O(log n) insert/extract.
- Cons: O(n) arbitrary search.

Use Cases:
- Priority queues.
- Scheduling.
- Graph algorithms (e.g., Dijkstra’s).

Complexity:
- Insert/Extract: O(log n)
- Peek: O(1)

Subtypes:
1. Min Heap:
   Definition: Heap where parent is less than or equal to children.
   Diagram:
   ```
           [ 1 ]
          /     \
         /       \
       [ 2 ]    [ 3 ]
      /     \
    [ 4 ]  [ 5 ]
   ```
2. Max Heap:
   Definition: Heap where parent is greater than or equal to children.
   Diagram:
   ```
           [ 5 ]
          /     \
         /       \
       [ 3 ]    [ 4 ]
      /     \
    [ 1 ]  [ 2 ]
   ```
3. Binary Heap:
   Definition: Complete binary tree implementing min or max heap.
   Diagram:
   ```
           [ 1 ]
          /     \
         /       \
       [ 2 ]    [ 3 ]
      /     \
    [ 4 ]  [ 5 ]
   ```
4. Fibonacci Heap:
   Definition: Heap with faster amortized operations using a forest of trees.
   Diagram:
   ```
   [ 1 ]→[ 2 ]
   |      |
 [ 3 ] [ 4 , 5 ]
   ```

title: Hashing
content:
Definition: Hashing is a technique mapping keys to values using a hash function for fast retrieval.

Key Points:
- Operations: insert, search, delete.
- Pros: O(1) average access.
- Cons: Collisions need resolution.

Use Cases:
- Dictionaries.
- Caching.
- Database indexing.

Complexity:
- Insert/Search/Delete: O(1) average, O(n) worst

Subtypes:
1. HashMap:
   Definition: Key-value store using hashing for O(1) average access.
   Diagram:
   ```
   [ 0 ]→[ k1:v1 ]
   [ 1 ]→[ k2:v2 ]
   ```
2. HashSet:
   Definition: Set of unique keys using hashing.
   Diagram:
   ```
   [ 0 ]→[ k1 ]
   [ 1 ]→[ k2 ]
   ```
3. Open Addressing:
   Definition: Resolves collisions by probing (e.g., linear probing).
   Diagram:
   ```
   [ 0:k1:v1 ] [ 1:k2:v2 ]
   ```
4. Chaining:
   Definition: Resolves collisions using linked lists per bucket.
   Diagram:
   ```
   [ 0 ]→[ k1:v1 ]→[ k2:v2 ]
   ```

title: Graphs
content:
Definition: A graph is a non-linear data structure of vertices (nodes) connected by edges.

Key Points:
- Operations: DFS, BFS, shortest path.
- Pros: Models relationships.
- Cons: High memory for dense graphs.

Use Cases:
- Social networks.
- Routing.
- Dependency graphs.

Complexity:
- BFS/DFS: O(V + E)
- Space: O(V + E) adjacency list

Subtypes:
1. Directed Graph:
   Definition: Graph with directed edges (one-way connections).
   Diagram:
   ```
      [ 1 ]→[ 2 ]
       ↓
      [ 3 ]
   ```
2. Undirected Graph:
   Definition: Graph with bidirectional edges.
   Diagram:
   ```
      [ 1 ]—[ 2 ]
       |
      [ 3 ]
   ```
3. Weighted Graph:
   Definition: Graph with weighted edges (e.g., distances).
   Diagram:
   ```
      [ 1 ]—5—[ 2 ]
       |
      3
      [ 3 ]
   ```
4. Unweighted Graph:
   Definition: Graph with no edge weights.
   Diagram:
   ```
      [ 1 ]—[ 2 ]
       |
      [ 3 ]
   ```
5. Cyclic Graph:
   Definition: Graph with at least one cycle.
   Diagram:
   ```
      [ 1 ]—[ 2 ]
       |    /
       [ 3 ]
   ```
6. Acyclic Graph:
   Definition: Graph with no cycles (e.g., DAG).
   Diagram:
   ```
      [ 1 ]→[ 2 ]→[ 3 ]
   ```
7. Adjacency List:
   Definition: Graph using lists of neighbors.
   Diagram:
   ```
   1: [ 2 , 3 ]
   2: [ 1 ]
   3: [ 1 ]
   ```
8. Adjacency Matrix:
   Definition: Graph using a 2D matrix.
   Diagram:
   ```
   [ 0  1  1 ]
   [ 1  0  0 ]
   [ 1  0  0 ]
   ```

title: Tries
content:
Definition: A trie (prefix tree) is a tree-like structure storing strings, where each node represents a character.

Key Points:
- Operations: insert, search, prefix search.
- Pros: Fast string lookups (O(m)).
- Cons: High memory usage.

Use Cases:
- Autocomplete.
- Spell checkers.
- Dictionaries.

Complexity:
- Insert/Search: O(m) (m = string length)

Subtypes:
1. Standard Trie:
   Definition: Trie where each node stores a single character.
   Diagram:
   ```
           [ * ]
          /     \
         /       \
       [ t ]     [ * ]
      /           |
    [ o ]        [ e ]
                  |
                 [ a ]
   ```
2. Compressed Trie:
   Definition: Trie merging single-child paths into one node.
   Diagram:
   ```
           [ * ]
          /     \
         /       \
      [ to ]   [ tea ]
   ```
3. Suffix Trie:
   Definition: Trie storing all suffixes of a string.
   Diagram:
   ```
           [ * ]
          / | \
         /  |  \
       [ a ][ b ][ c ]
   ```

title: Sets
content:
Definition: A set is a collection of unique elements, disallowing duplicates.

Key Points:
- Operations: insert, erase, find.
- Pros: Fast lookup, deduplication.
- Cons: No indexed access.

Use Cases:
- Membership testing.
- Removing duplicates.
- Set operations.

Complexity:
- Insert/Erase/Find: O(log n) ordered, O(1) unordered

Subtypes:
1. HashSet:
   Definition: Unordered set using hashing for O(1) average access.
   Diagram:
   ```
   [ 0 ]→[ 1 ]
   [ 1 ]→[ 2 ]
   ```
2. TreeSet:
   Definition: Ordered set using a balanced tree for sorted access.
   Diagram:
   ```
           [ 2 ]
          /     \
         /       \
       [ 1 ]    [ 3 ]
   ```
3. UnorderedSet:
   Definition: Hash-based set with no order guarantee.
   Diagram:
   ```
   [ 0 ]→[ 1 ]
   [ 1 ]→[ 2 ]
   ```
4. OrderedSet:
   Definition: Sorted set maintaining element order.
   Diagram:
   ```
           [ 2 ]
          /     \
         /       \
       [ 1 ]    [ 3 ]
   ```

title: Maps
content:
Definition: A map is a collection of key-value pairs, where each key is unique.

Key Points:
- Operations: insert, erase, find by key.
- Pros: Fast key-based lookup.
- Cons: Key memory overhead.

Use Cases:
- Dictionaries.
- Caching.
- Associative arrays.

Complexity:
- Insert/Erase/Find: O(log n) ordered, O(1) unordered

Subtypes:
1. HashMap:
   Definition: Unordered map using hashing for O(1) average access.
   Diagram:
   ```
   [ 0 ]→[ k1:v1 ]
   [ 1 ]→[ k2:v2 ]
   ```
2. TreeMap:
   Definition: Ordered map using a balanced tree for sorted keys.
   Diagram:
   ```
           [ k2 ]
          /      \
         /        \
       [ k1 ]   [ k3 ]
   ```
3. OrderedMap:
   Definition: Map with sorted keys, similar to TreeMap.
   Diagram:
   ```
           [ k2 ]
          /      \
         /        \
       [ k1 ]   [ k3 ]
   ```
4. UnorderedMap:
   Definition: Hash-based map with no key order.
   Diagram:
   ```
   [ 0 ]→[ k1:v1 ]
   [ 1 ]→[ k2:v2 ]
   ```

title: Matrices
content:
Definition: A matrix is a two-dimensional array of elements, typically numbers, arranged in rows and columns.

Key Points:
- Operations: access, update, traversal, matrix operations.
- Pros: Structured data representation.
- Cons: High memory for large matrices.

Use Cases:
- Graph adjacency.
- Image processing.
- Linear algebra.

Complexity:
- Access: O(1)
- Traversal: O(m*n) (m rows, n columns)

Subtypes:
1. Diagonal Matrix:
   Definition: Matrix with non-zero elements only on the main diagonal.
   Diagram:
   ```
   [ 1  0  0 ]
   [ 0  2  0 ]
   [ 0  0  3 ]
   ```
2. Sparse Matrix:
   Definition: Matrix with mostly zeros, optimized for non-zero storage.
   Diagram:
   ```
   [ 0  0  7 ]
   [ 0  0  0 ]
   [ 9  0  0 ]
   ```
3. Symmetric Matrix:
   Definition: Square matrix where element (i,j) equals (j,i).
   Diagram:
   ```
   [ 1  2  3 ]
   [ 2  4  5 ]
   [ 3  5  6 ]
   ```
4. Identity Matrix:
   Definition: Square matrix with 1s on main diagonal, 0s elsewhere.
   Diagram:
   ```
   [ 1  0  0 ]
   [ 0  1  0 ]
   [ 0  0  1 ]
   ```
